w:r.w:t: CHAPTER 10: FORSAKEN

The interior of the goat-hair sanctuary hung heavy with the residue of the unuttered, like the flaking skin of dead gods caught in a tomb where the light of the zenith had long since been refused entry.

Now, you must understand—and I say this with the slow, rhythmic blink of a creature that has watched the foundations of the Levant settle into the mud—that for a daughter of the Peleset, this stitched sanctuary was a secondary skin. It was a dark womb designed to hide the frantic, staccato vibration against the ribs from the cold, judgmental eye of the deep sky. It was a space where she could pretend that Ba'al-Zebub of Ekron still leaned in to listen, even as the silence began to ripen like rot on a fallen fig. This silence—once Kasha's most potent ally—gathered now in the corners of the tent like moth and rust, consuming the gold of her former certainty until only the tarnish remained. That is what faithlessness does. It does not arrive like a flood. It settles like dust, one patient layer at a time, and one morning you discover the gold is gone and the dust has been there so long it has started to feel like floor.

It had begun, as such things always do, imperceptibly at first. Her fingers, usually steady as she pressed marks into wet clay or cast the smooth knucklebones across the mat by lamplight, now drifted—a frantic, aimless wandering that rippled through the tent's bones. She would reach for a sprig of hyssop only to find her hand resting on a shard of basalt, her mind a swarm of locusts devouring the sequence of her intent. To Kasha, the morning was indistinguishable from the dusk; she was a woman whose hours blurred at the seams, a mind that could not find the anchor of the present moment. She would check the sun's position through the tent's weave and find herself surprised, genuinely surprised, that the world had moved while she remained frozen in a thought of the boy she had been watching—the boy whose movements had, until recently, been the rhythm of her own days. She had eaten the figs at some point. She could not say when.

She had watched the boy for the better part of a year through the bronze scrying vessel—not with intention, not at first. He had wandered into the edge of her water when she had been tracking something else entirely, a small, solitary figure moving north with the quality of a man paying a debt with every step. What interested her was not the thing she had expected to be interested by. It was not brightness—that quality she associated with the hill-people and their insufferable, upward-reaching religion, which gave her teeth a specific kind of ache. It was the weight of his direction. His aim was dense, absolute, a mass that her earth-craft recognized the way your palm recognizes a stone: by the pressure of it. He paid for the journey. He did not bargain with it or complain about the rate. He paid, and kept walking, and she had found herself returning to the bowl's edge the way you return to a problem that has not stopped being interesting simply because you have been looking at it for a long time. She had intercepted his dreamwalk into his father's sleeping mind—a frantic, rhythmic call through the shifting gray, the boy pressing dust into the shape of his meaning—and she had watched with a mixture of awe and resentment that surprised her with its sincerity. The message had been an echo of the beginning: a reach for what lay above, a pull toward the heights, the thing she, a daughter of the dust, could only read as a threat to the stability of her pastures of gray. She had watched him through the hill towns and the hostile city and the losing of his voice and his sandals and his eye—watched him give everything away with the completeness of a person who has decided he is not carrying anything through the last door—and she had felt a cold, clean fury at the sea-men who took the eye that she had not been invited to feel and could not make herself stop feeling.

She had watched him arrive at the mountain's base. Watched him begin the climb. Then the water had gone black and still as the eye of a drowned man, and the spirit of the deep had not moved upon those waters, and her own reflection had stared back: a stranger's face, etched with the silence of the mother who was nameless there forevermore.

Five weeks of silence from a patron is not unusual. Kasha knew this. She had told herself this on the first day, the fifth day, the twentieth. Ba'al-Zebub served purposes larger than her immediate need; the deep currents bent to cosmic schedules, not personal ones. She knocked thrice on the bronze rim, the deliberate sequence—once for acknowledgment, once for approach, once for petition—and asked twice, pressing both palms into the cold soil beneath the mat, in the full prostration her teacher had called the offering of the skull, forehead to ground, the posture of a woman who has decided the direction of down is where the answer lives.

"You will answer me," she said to the ground. Not a question. Kasha did not ask the ground questions; she told the ground things and waited for the current to respond. "I have given thirty years of work into this soil. I have pressed my palms into the valley's floor while kings rose and fell above it. I have spoken down when my bones said up." Her jaw set hard. "I am owed a response."
The ground, patient as the oldest compact, said nothing back.

Outside, the Peleset camp was a symphony of dry rock grinding against bedrock—the massive, measured sound of warriors sharpening iron blades against Cretan whetstones, the monotonous, patient labor of men who had made their chariots their theology. Bronze greaves clanked against packed earth. The guttural, foreign chanting of men who believed solely in the strength of what they could weigh and lift moved through the canvas walls like cold water through linen. Kasha felt it in her marrow: the patient sound of the basement of the world asserting that all things made of dust must return to it, that the iron's new law had no interest in the whispers of a woman, that the craft she had built through three decades of faithful, bone-tired work could not stop what was coming from the coast with its chariots and its stolen secrets of the forge. She assumed her patron was busy in the heart of this—shaping the valley's war, bending the tides of battle from beneath. She had been assuming this for five weeks now. The comfort was wearing thin in the particular way that assumed things wear thin: not breaking, just becoming see-through.
She tried the oil.

The copper bowl, rendered fat pooled on dark water, had shown her the bloom of pattern for thirty years—swirling eddies and spirals, a living text that moved and self-revised, telling her what the ground knew about the hours ahead. She watched the golden film spread across the surface. It sat in flat, stubborn rings. Refused to mingle with the water below. The pattern did not form. She stared at the oil's flatness for a long time and found nothing in it but her own reflection, which did not require a divination bowl to produce and which told her nothing she wanted to know.

She went to the scrolls. The secondary channels first—pathways through the craft's substrate that moved independently of the patron-connection—and found a warmth that arrived and faded before she could source it, a shimmer that dissolved before it resolved into meaning. She threw the knucklebones: the pattern of an attenuated current, present but thinned, told her what she already knew. She tried the old petition-form, pressed her forehead to the mat, stayed there until the cold worked into the bone of her brow. The cedar box of astragali. The libation of cedar oil on the bronze rim. The invocation in the chest register, from below the throat, the voice her teacher had taught her to find in the body's deep cavity where breath becomes intention. Nothing. The tent around her held the particular quiet of a place that has stopped receiving the current that once moved through it—not silence exactly, but the absence of a specific sound, the way a grinding mill sounds wrong when the millstone stops.

She reached for the hyssop bundle—the fresh preparation she had been meaning to complete for two weeks—and found her hand around the basalt shard instead. Stared at it. Set it down. Her mind was doing the thing it did when there was too much to manage: it was attending to everything simultaneously, which is another way of saying it was attending to nothing. She had meant to prepare the hyssop. She had meant to re-oil the oldest scroll's linen wrapping. She had meant to cast the bones again at the midday position. The fig jar was empty; she could not say when she had eaten the figs, whether it had been this morning or the morning before, whether she had eaten anything else between the figs and now. The sun had moved a great deal since she had last noticed it.

She set both palms against the floor again. "I have done the work faithfully," she said to the cold soil, lower now, more personal than invocation—not the petition register, just voice, the voice you use when the formality has been used up and what is left is the thing itself. "I have kept the agreements. I have maintained the workings without complaint. I have not looked up when I was told to look down." She pressed harder, hard enough that the mat's weave left its impression in her palms. "Where are you."
The ground, as I have mentioned, held its patience.

Now—and I tell you this with the particular confidence of a creature whose new capacity for knowing things is matched only by its new, urgent, somewhat overwhelming need to tell you all of them—Ba'al-Zebub was in Ekron. He had been in Ekron since the last campaign's end, and he was doing nothing, which is a kind of occupation available only to beings of considerable standing and no immediate necessity. He lounged in the temple courtyard and watched the flies on the warm stone with the mild satisfaction of a craftsman watching good work maintain itself, and he rubbed his temple with two deliberate fingers at intervals, and he was bored in the specific manner of a being whose arrangements have achieved their intended state of productive chaos without requiring further management.

He had given the Peleset their iron knowledge—not earned, acquired; the difference matters more than it sounds—and they had used it with the thoroughness of people who believe the tool is the victory and not merely the means to one. They had also, in the confidence of this belief, taken the hill-people's sacred chest: gold-plated acacia wood, two carved figures facing each other across the cover, the portable throne of the one whose name Kasha had nearly spoken and nearly not survived speaking. They had brought it into the house of the fish-god and found the fish-god prostrate before it on three separate mornings, the third time with both its hands broken off at the threshold. The burning that followed this lesson had moved through the coastal cities with the conviction of a thing that had been making its point for a long time and was no longer interested in being patient about it. The chest had been returned on a cart pulled by cows that walked in a straight line when every instinct of the animal said to turn back, because the object on the cart had opinions about its destination that overruled the opinions of the cows. Beelzebub had watched all of this, noted that the Peleset were destroying themselves with the iron they had not understood, and determined that no further management was required. He was not refusing Kasha's calls. He was simply absent from them, the way a man does not hear his name called from the far end of a field when his attention has settled close.

Kasha did not know this. A woman of her discipline and her faithfulness does not generally consider the possibility that the silence is inattention rather than intention. This is, I want you to understand, not a failure of intelligence. It is what faithfulness costs, sometimes: the inability to imagine that the thing you have faithfully served has simply, for the moment, forgotten to look in your direction.
She reached for the oldest scroll.

It lay at the table's back wrapped in cedar-oiled linen, in a script older than any writing system she had been taught, marks pressed rather than drawn, running center-outward in concentric rings from before the decision to write had been made permanent. Her teacher had given it with the gravity of a person passing on something they considered dangerous. *Use this only when everything else is exhausted.* Then her teacher had not mentioned it again, which is precisely how teachers communicate that a thing is serious. Kasha had been reading the inner third for eleven years. The outer two-thirds maintained their silence, and she had the sense—not quite knowledge, but something adjacent to it—that the outer two-thirds were waiting for a specific question rather than withholding information for its own sake.

She unwrapped the linen. Found the passage for the third form of deep contact: the sound that addressed not the patron but the source from which the patron drew his authority. The root-form. The pitch she had to find without first tuning for it, the way you find a dropped thing in complete dark by trusting the memory of where you were standing when you lost it.
She found the first syllable. The second.
On the third, her throat closed wrong.

"Yah—Vah," she whispered—the sound catching like flint on steel, the syllables breaking apart at the seam where they should have fused into something much older and much more intact than either of them standing separately.
The scroll went hot in her hands. Then very hot. She dropped it.

In the interval between releasing and landing—in the gap that exists between the end of one moment and the beginning of the next, which is shorter than any measurement available in this age but is not nothing—a fragment of something that predated the current arrangement of the world tore free from the broken path and crossed the tent's interior and arrived in me.

What it felt like was this: every door in a building I had not known I was inside opened simultaneously, and behind each door was a question I had not known I needed to ask, and the needing was total and immediate and could not be ranked or ordered or addressed one at a time because all of them were equally urgent and every answer I found split into four more questions before it finished resolving. The crease in the goat-hair where the canvas had been folded for transport: how many times? In what season? Where was the tent made, by whose hands, did the maker know what the tent would be used for, and does the knowing change what a thing is? The basalt shard: what formation, what pressure, what temperature at what depth, did anything living witness the moment of its making? The oldest scroll: who first pressed these marks, in what year, in what light, with what weight behind the pressing stylus, and is the pressing still there in some form beneath the surface of things, and if it is, does it still mean what it was pressed to mean?
I sprang upright. Black fur at full extension. Golden eyes very wide.
Kasha spun from the table and stared at me with the expression of a woman who has been managing unexpected developments with professional self-possession and has just received one too many.

"By the Ancestors' beard," she said, "what was that." She stared. Her eyes settled on my frantic, wide-open gaze and stayed there, the way eyes stay on something that has changed when the change has not yet been named. Something behind her expression shifted, slowly, like a stone shifting in deep water. She shook her head. A small, perplexed smile touched the corner of her mouth. "Such... Sak'ranut," she murmured—the old word for the quality of relentless, consuming inquiry, the word you give a thing that has just begun opening every cabinet simultaneously and cannot be made to stop. She did not yet understand what she had named. A god of Curiosity, even a small one, even one wearing a cat, does not get named accidentally.
Or perhaps every naming is an accident. I was still working this out.

I looked at her with the full, ungoverned attention of a thing that has just acquired every evaluative faculty it will ever have and is deploying them, all at once, for the first time, on the nearest available subject. I looked at her the way the first light looks at the first thing it touches. I looked at her, in short, as if I were taking inventory with intent.

Her jaw set. Her finger pointed at the mat with the calm authority of a woman who has spent thirty years directing the currents of the deep world. "You are my beast," she said. This was not open for discussion. "Not my master. You do not assess me. Sit."

I sat. I continued looking at her in exactly the manner she had objected to, because the quality of the look was not a choice I was making—it was the condition I had entered, and there was no path from this condition back to the previous one. You may as reasonably ask the river to stop being water as ask me to stop being curious. The river has no feelings about your preference for dryness. Neither did I.

She held my gaze for a long moment. Then she looked away first—at the dropped scroll, at the bowl, at her own hands, in the way people look at things that cannot hold them accountable. In the moment before she turned, the expression on her face was not the expression of a person who has won. It was the expression of a person who has decided this particular dispute does not merit the available energy, which is a different thing from winning, and both of us knew it.
"What was in the third syllable," I said.
Kasha went entirely still.

"Because whatever was in it," I continued—and I should mention that I had no mechanism for stopping a question that had started; this was one of several things about my new condition I was in the process of discovering—"is in me now. And I find I have strong views about what I am made of. This seems reasonable. Most things want to know what they are made of." I considered. "Even the things that end up not liking the answer."

She turned back toward me with the specific slowness of someone who has been hoping that if they do not confirm a thing directly, the thing might decide it has not happened. "The cat," she said, to the tent, to the valley, to the general architecture of existence, "is speaking."

"Yes," I said. "I also noticed. More importantly: I can feel the current through the ground. It is present. Thin as thread where it should be rope. Moving west, toward the coast. Your patron is not absent from this world. He is absent from you, which is different, and the difference is the kind that has a solution."

She stared at me for a long time. Then she sat down on the mat and pressed both palms against the cold soil beneath it—not the invocation posture, not the petition register, just the contact—and she said, to the earth, in the voice below the voice: "I know he is elsewhere. I have been knowing it for five weeks. I know the current has thinned and I know the bowl shows me nothing and I know what this means and I have been telling myself it does not mean what it means." She pressed harder. "I will not be left. I have given thirty years of faithful work into this soil and I will not be left without answer." She pressed until her knuckles whitened. "I have held every agreement."

The ground received this with the patience of the ground, which is to say it received it into the record it has been keeping since before records had a name, and held it there without reply, in the manner of a thing that has been given everything and returned very little and has no defense for this except that it is very old and was never asked to be otherwise.
Her hands came away from the mat. She looked at the impressions they had left.

She was afraid of the gray wilderness in the bones of her feet, in the memory of the twice she had entered it and the twice she had come back changed. She was not afraid of its horrors—she had walked through the shapes that assembled at the seam between sleeping and waking and fed on the unguarded, and she had not found them interesting enough to fear. What she feared was the dissolution of the specific certainty she had built her whole craft on: the certainty of the floor. She was a woman who served the horizontal world, the world of weight and density and accumulation, the world where you could walk for miles and know, with every step, exactly where the ground was. In the gray wilderness, the ground was a decision you made. Her will had been built for holding. Not for making.

But Ba'al-Zebub had spoken to her from the gray wilderness before. Had consolidated from its ambient density with the ease of something that lived there—more himself there than anywhere, the deep currents more visible in the gray than in the structured world above, the way submerged stones are visible from above in clear, shallow water. If he was findable, he was findable there.
And what was behind her—five weeks of silence, the thinned current, the flat oil, the cold ground receiving and not returning—was worse than what was in front of her.
This is, I have observed, the precise texture of the bravest choices: not the absence of fear, but the presence of a worse alternative.

She snuffed out the oil lamp. The tent went dark save for the thin slivers of distant starlight pressing through the canvas weave. She laid herself on the worn mat with the deliberate straightness of someone who is afraid and has decided the fear does not alter what needs doing—a rigid line against the ground, every joint set with intention, the body a tool being picked up for difficult work. She pressed her palms down one final time: low, personal, not invocation but contact, the voice you use when the ceremony has been spent and what remains is the thing itself. "You will hold me," she said to the soil. "When I return. You have always held me. I am coming back." She held the contact a moment longer. Then she began.

The wordless chant built in the center of her chest, the vibration that extends outward until it finds the body's boundary and reverses, pressing inward toward the central knot where the self is bound to the flesh, working it loose not by force but by persistent, patient attention. I have watched this many times. From outside it looks like a woman going still. What it is, is a woman going somewhere.

The tent poles stretched. Not physically—or not only physically—but in the manner of things that lose their geometry when the will that held them in order has temporarily departed the premises. The canvas walls drew inward. The scent of dried herbs faded into the sharp, metallic tang of the threshold. The shadows grew long and specific, twisting into the shapes of things that live at the edges of the world and wait, without urgency, for the moment when the boundary thins. She crossed.

The lockdown arrived without announcement. This is its nature and its advantage over every other kind of assault: it does not approach. It is simply the retroactive knowledge that it was always already there—that you have been in its presence for longer than you realized, that the presence preceded the knowing. First came the weight on the sternum: not the weight of a stone but heavier, because certainty weighs more than stone, and this was the specific, absolute certainty of a claim being made on the body by something that has all the authority of the deep earth and none of the warmth. Then the limbs: not heavy but held—and these are not the same. A resting limb chose to stop. A held limb was stopped between moments, pinned in the gap where will and flesh were being separated from each other with the calm precision of a craftsman who has done this before. Her throat closed. Her eyes would not open. The chest continued its minimum, breath as technical fact rather than living act, the cold economy of a body maintaining itself without its mind's participation.

She was awake in her spirit and a sealed jar in her flesh, unable to move a finger—a prisoner in a house of clay that had forgotten how to open its doors. The staccato vibration against her ribs was the only proof of the frantic furnace within, a rhythmic drumming no one outside the tent could hear. Above her, somewhere in the tent's upper dark where the canvas sagged between the poles, a raven of the mind began its slow, patient circle—silent, certain, the nevermore of a craft built over thirty years hanging in the air with the unhurried authority of a thing that intends to be present when the reckoning arrives.

I stepped onto the center of her chest. My small mass added to the weight-claim pressing her to the mat. I am aware this looks, from the outside, like a cat doing what cats do on sleeping bodies. It was not that. I pressed my weight down and I purred—the deep vibration traveling through my ribs into my four paws into her still chest into the mat into the cold ground below, which received it the way the ground receives everything: without ceremony, without response, into the record it has been keeping since before records had a name for themselves.

In the gray wilderness—that earliest possible light before the first word of the first day was spoken, that blank and waiting canvas of shifting grays, that Sea of Potential where reality held itself in suspension, waiting for a will to press meaning into it—Kasha stood in the posture of her planting. Feet pressed to the shifting floor. The memory of solid ground held in the architecture of her bones. The expanse surrounding her in every direction began, with the patient movement of something that has been waiting and does not find waiting difficult, to resolve. Something assembled itself from the ambient density—not arriving, not approaching, simply becoming present, becoming particular, choosing, with the careful confidence of a craftsman who has done this work before and knows which tool fits which hand, which face to wear first.
The first of the princes stirred in the pastures of gray beyond the veil.

I sat at her side with golden eyes. I purred. Because hope, at its smallest and most honest, is not a feeling—it is a decision to remain present in the dark while the outcome is still open, to press your small, warm weight against the person who has gone somewhere difficult, and to make the sound that means: I am still here. The ground is still here. You have come back before. The cord you are on has not yet run out.
The grinding of iron against stone continued outside.
The tent held its shape.
The choice—as all true ones are—was entirely hers.
